q: A capacitor is used as an electric circuit component to store electrical energy.
The capacitor was originally known as a condenser.
A capacitor consists of two conductors separated by a large distance.
Film capacitors use plastic film as the dielectric medium.
Film capacitors are available with working voltages up to $1500\text{ V}$.
Ceramic capacitors use an oxide layer as the dielectric material.
Ceramic capacitors are mainly used for high stability performances with low losses.
The capacitance values of ceramic capacitors are stable with respect to the applied voltage, frequency and temperature.
Electrolytic capacitors have a narrow tolerance capacity.
Tantalum and aluminum are the two main types of electrolytic capacitors.
The failure of electrolytic capacitors can be hazardous, resulting in an explosion or fire.
Variable capacitors mostly use air as the dielectric medium.
Variable capacitors are mostly used in AC circuits especially for high frequency, high power and low loss.
A supercapacitor (SC) is also called an ultracapacitor.
Supercapacitors have higher voltage limits than other capacitors.
A supercapacitor typically stores 10 to 100 times more energy than electrolytic capacitors.
Supercapacitors tolerate many more charge and discharge cycles than rechargeable batteries.
Supercapacitors are used for regenerative braking in automobiles, buses, and trains.
Smaller supercapacitor units are used as power backup for static random-access memory (SRAM).
Film capacitors and ceramic capacitors are polarized capacitors.
Polarized capacitors do not have positive and negative terminals.
Capacitors are used for blocking DC current after getting fully charged but allow the AC current to pass through it.
Capacitors are used to eliminate sparking in automobile ignition systems.
Capacitors can be used as sensors for measuring humidity, fuel levels, and mechanical strain.
Any flashing light or regular beeping likely uses a timing capacitor.
When a capacitor is connected to a battery, the two conductors have charges of equal magnitude and opposite signs.
The magnitude of the charge on each conductor, $Q$, is called the charge of the capacitor.
The quantity of charge $Q$ on the capacitor is inversely proportional to the potential difference $V$ between the conductors.
The relationship between charge and potential difference is written as $Q = CV$.
The capacitance of a capacitor is defined as the ratio of the potential difference to the charge.
In the SI system, the unit of capacitance is farad (F).
One farad (F) is equivalent to $1\text{ C V}^{-1}$.
The unit farad is named in honor of Michael Faraday.
The farad is a very small unit of capacitance.
Typical devices have capacitances ranging from microfarad ($\mu\text{F}$) to picofarad ($\text{pF}$).
One nanofarad (nF) is equal to $10^{-12}\text{ F}$.
When the charge on the capacitor is increased, the capacitance remains constant.
The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the size and the shape of the capacitor.
The capacitance of a capacitor depends on the nature of the insulator between the two conductors.
The electric capacity is the amount of charge given to a conductor to change its potential by one unit.
One picofarad (pF) is equal to $10^{-9}\text{ F}$.
If the charge on a capacitor is increased, the potential difference also increases proportionally.
The SI unit for electric capacity is the farad.
One microfarad ($\mu\text{F}$) is equal to $10^{-6}\text{ F}$.
The capacitance $C$ is the ratio of charge $Q$ to potential difference $V$.
A parallel-plate capacitor consists of two parallel metal plates separated by an insulator.
Charging a capacitor means connecting it to a battery.
Surface charge density $\sigma$ is defined as charge per unit surface area on each plate.
If the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor are very close together, the electric field between them is uniform.
The electric field $E$ between the plates can be expressed as $E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon}$.
The magnitude of the potential difference $V$ between the plates equals $Ed$.
The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is directly proportional to the separation of the plates.
The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is directly proportional to the area of its plates.
The permittivity $\epsilon$ of an insulating material is related to vacuum permittivity $\epsilon_0$ as $\epsilon = \kappa \epsilon_0$.
The symbol $\kappa$ represents the dielectric constant or relative permittivity of the insulating material.
The permittivity of vacuum $\epsilon_0$ is approximately $8.85 \times 10^{-12}\text{ C}^2\text{ N}^{-1}\text{ m}^{-2}$.
For vacuum, the dielectric constant $\kappa$ is 1.
For insulating materials other than vacuum, the dielectric constant $\kappa$ is less than 1.
The dielectric constant is defined as the ratio of the permittivity of a material to that of a vacuum.
The ratio of the capacitance of a capacitor with an insulator to the capacitance with a vacuum is called the dielectric constant.
Dielectric constant can be expressed as the ratio of potential differences $V_0/V$.
When a charged capacitor is disconnected from a battery and a dielectric is inserted, the potential difference increases.
Insulating materials have values of dielectric constant $\kappa$ greater than unity.
A dielectric provides the advantage of increasing the maximum operating voltage.
A dielectric provides mechanical support between the plates, allowing them to be close together without electrical contact.
The dielectric strength of insulating materials is less than that of air.
A dielectric increases the maximum operating voltage even though the physical size of the capacitor doesn't change.
Inserting a dielectric between plates results in a decrease in capacitance $C$.
The dielectric constant $\kappa$ for water at $18^{\circ}\text{C}$ is 81.
The dielectric constant for air at 1 atm is exactly 1.
A capacitor stores electrical energy in the form of an electric field.
The charging process is viewed as the apparent transfer of charge between two conductors.
Work is done to transfer charge from a conductor at higher potential to one at lower potential.
The energy stored by a capacitor is equal to the area under the curve of a $Q-V$ graph.
The work done in charging a capacitor is equal to $QV$.
Before a capacitor is charged, the potential difference between its two conductors is zero.
The average potential difference of the capacitor during the charging process is $\frac{V}{2}$.
The energy of a capacitor can be expressed as $W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
Placing a resistor in the charging circuit speeds up the charging process.
The greater the values of resistance and capacitance, the longer it takes to charge the capacitor.
The product of the resistance $R$ and the capacitance $C$ is called the time constant $\tau$.
The time constant $\tau$ is measured in seconds.
During the charging process, the capacitor voltage falls off with time.
In the discharging process, the voltage of the capacitor falls off with time.
A capacitor is discharged through a load resistor by opening switch $S_1$ and closing switch $S_2$.
Capacitors in parallel all have the same potential difference.
In a parallel combination, all capacitors carry the same amount of charge.
The equivalent capacitance of capacitors connected in parallel is the sum of the individual capacitances.
For $n$ identical capacitors in parallel, the equivalent capacitance $C_p$ is $nC$.
In a series combination, the negatively charged plate of one capacitor is connected to the positively charged plate of another.
Capacitors connected in series each have the same charge.
In a series combination, the capacitors have the same potential difference.
The reciprocal of the equivalent capacitance in series is the sum of the reciprocals of the individual capacitances.
For $n$ identical capacitors in series, the equivalent capacitance is $C_s = \frac{C}{n}$.
The magnitude of charge on the series combination is equal to the sum of the charges on individual capacitors.
The total potential difference of a series combination is the sum of the individual potential differences.
In parallel combination, the total charge $Q$ is the sum of individual charges $Q_1, Q_2,$ and $Q_3$.
Equivalent capacitance is also called the capacitance of a single capacitor that replaces the combination.
The equivalent capacitance of a series combination is always larger than any individual capacitance in the series.
The equivalent capacitance of a parallel combination is the sum of the individual capacitances.
